source.module: Core Django Fundamentals – Templates & Static Content

topicTags: Core Django Fundamentals – Templates & Static Content

content.problemStatement:
In Django, template inheritance is a powerful feature that allows you to define a common structure for your website using base templates, and then build upon that structure using child templates. This keeps your code DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) and makes it easy to manage layout consistency across multiple pages.
____

#### What is a Base Template?
A base template is a skeleton HTML file that contains common elements shared across different pages of your site — such as the header, navigation bar, and footer. It also defines content blocks where child templates can insert page-specific content.

For example, let’s create a **base.html** in your project's templates directory:
```html




    {% block title %}My Website{% endblock %}


    
        Welcome to My Website
    
    
    
        
            Home
            About
        
    
    
    
        {% block content %}
        
        {% endblock %}
    
    
    
        © 2025 My Website
    


```
In this template, we’ve defined two blocks using **{% block %}** tags:

- `{% block title %}` for the page title

- `{% block content %}` for the main content area

You can also define additional blocks to allow more granular customization of other layout sections:

- `{% block header %}`: For customizing or replacing the header section (e.g., different banners or welcome messages per page)

- `{% block navigation %}`: Useful if some pages require different navigation menus or links

- `{% block footer %}`: Allows child templates to modify or extend the footer with additional links, disclaimers, or page-specific details

These blocks will be overridden by child templates.

#### Extending Templates with Child Templates
Once you have a base template, you can create other templates that extend it. This is done using the {% extends %} tag at the very beginning of the child template.

Here’s an example of a child template called **home.html** that extends **base.html**:

```html

{% extends "base.html" %}

{% block title %}Home - My Website{% endblock %}

{% block content %}
Welcome to the homepage!
This is the main content for the homepage.
{% endblock %}
```

When Django renders **home.html**, it uses the structure from **base.html**, but replaces the content inside the title and content blocks with what’s provided in **home.html**.

**Benefits of Template Inheritance**
- Reusability: Shared layout elements are written only once.

- Consistency: All pages follow the same design framework.

- Maintainability: Changes to the layout need to be made only in the base template.
